competencies.competency_text: Observes and identifies the natural (insects, plants, birds, animals, geographical features, sun and moon, stars, planets, natural resources) and social (houses, relationships) components in their immediate environment
competencies.justification: 'Here Comes the Rain!' requires students to directly observe rainfall — noting intensity, drop size, direction, and cleanliness of collected rainwater. 'What happened to the rainwater?' extends this to identifying streams, rivers, ponds, lakes, and wells as natural water-body components in the environment.

competencies.competency_text: Asks questions and makes predictions about simple patterns (season change, food chain, phases of the moon, movement of stars and planets, shapes of trees, plants, leaves, and flowers, rituals, celebrations) observed in the immediate environment
competencies.justification: 'Here Comes the Rain!' explicitly instructs students to watch the sky daily for a week, predict whether it will rain, record clues, and guess why rain falls at an angle — a direct pattern-prediction task anchored in daily sky observation.

competencies.competency_text: Identifies natural and humanmade systems that support their lives (water supply, water cycle, river flow systems, seasons, life cycle of plants and animals, food, household items, transport, communication, electricity in the home)
competencies.justification: 'Water is a Precious Gift!' traces the water supply system from tap → pipe → rooftop tank → municipal supply → well/borewell/tanker, directly matching the C-code's demand to identify human-made systems that support life.

competencies.competency_text: Connects changes in the environment and the lives of their family and community, as communicated by elders and through local stories (changes in occupation, food habits, resources, celebrations, communication)
competencies.justification: 'Water in Our Daily Lives' requires students to interview grandparents or elders about how they used to get and store water, what vessels they used, and how practices have changed — directly connecting past resource-use to present life through elder knowledge.

competencies.competency_text: Demonstrates how natural resources can be shared, maintained, and conserved (trees, use of rainwater, benefits of millets)
competencies.justification: 'Every Drop Counts' demonstrates conservation by instructing students to reduce and reuse water (watering plants, flushing with waste water) and explicitly presents the Indian tradition of keeping water pots outside for passersby as a water-sharing practice.

competencies.competency_text: Identifies needs of plants, birds, and animals, and how they can be supported (water, soil, food, care)
competencies.justification: 'Every Drop Counts' — Activity 4 requires students to build a birdbath, identify that birds need water to drink and cool down in summer, and maintain it regularly — a concrete act of identifying and supporting an animal's water need.

competencies.competency_text: Performs simple inquiry related to specific questions independently or in groups
competencies.justification: 'Here Comes the Rain!' — Activity 1 is a structured inquiry where students ask multiple specific questions about rain (intensity, drop size, direction, cleanliness) and collect data by observing and gathering rainwater in a vessel.

competencies.competency_text: Presents observations and findings through different creative modes (drawing, diagram, poem, play, skit, oral and written expression)
competencies.justification: 'Here Comes the Rain!' and 'Every Drop Counts' require students to draw a picture of observed rain, write observation notes, draw the birdbath with visiting birds, and write findings about local water bodies — presenting inquiry findings through drawing and written expression.
